title: Unlocking Feature Visualization for Deep Network with MAgnitude Constrained Optimization
abstract:
Feature visualization has gained significant popularity as an explainability method, particularly after the influential work by Olah et al. in 2017. Despite its success, its widespread adoption has been limited due to issues in scaling to deeper neural networks and the reliance on tricks to generate interpretable images. Here, we describe MACO, a simple approach to address these shortcomings. It consists in optimizing solely an image's phase spectrum while keeping its magnitude constant to ensure that the generated explanations lie in the space of natural images. Our approach yields significantly better results -- both qualitatively and quantitatively -- unlocking efficient and interpretable feature visualizations for state-of-the-art neural networks. We also show that our approach exhibits an attribution mechanism allowing to augment feature visualizations with spatial importance. Furthermore, we enable quantitative evaluation of feature visualizations by introducing 3 metrics: transferability, plausibility, and alignment with natural images. We validate our method on various applications and we introduce a website featuring MACO visualizations for all classes of the ImageNet dataset, which will be made available upon acceptance. 
Overall, our study unlocks feature visualizations for the largest, state-of-the-art classification networks without resorting to any parametric prior image model, effectively advancing a field that has been stagnating since 2017 (Olah et al, 2017).

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper proposes MACO, a feature visualization technique that optimizes image phase but keeps the magnitude of the Fourier spectrum constant. This method is similar, in spirit, to the 'classic' feature visualizations by Olah et al. (2017), while at the same time enabling feature visualizations for architectures where the classic approach previously struggled with (such as vision transformers or other non-VGG architectures). The paper lists three core contributions:

1. MACO, the new feature visualization technique,
2. Combining feature visualization with feature attribution,
3. Quantitative metrics to measure the success of the proposed method

Overall, (1.) can be seen as the most significant contribution whereas (2.) is an application and (3.) is a supporting contribution.

STRENGTHS:
1. The paper is very well written and a pleasure to read
2. The proposed approach, MACO, is refreshingly simple (e.g., fewer transformations needed to make it work) and has a clear motivation from human perception (importance of phase spectrum for human perception).
3. Making feature visualizations work for a broader range of architectures is a desirable goal, and there hasn't been much progress in this direction over the last few years
4. The authors make the findings explorable via a website (currently screenshots in the Appendix)
5. The paper contains a quantitative evaluation for at least some findings. The 'plausibility score' and the 'FID score' both make a very big assumption (natural images = best, which may not be the case for the model: in fact, in the introduction MACO is proposed exactly since the feature visualizations based on generative models like GANs make this assumption). Nevertheless, in a field that's predominantly evaluated 'by eye' (nice-looking visualizations) it's great that they perform at least some quantitative evaluations.

WEAKNESSES:
1. The strongest contribution is MACO itself. The approach of combining attribution and feature visualization, on the other hand, sounds like a great idea if at least one of those methods, and ideally both, would be reliable. In particular, many attribution methods are deeply problematic [1,2]. The particular method employed in the paper is similar to SmoothGrad which often produces attributions that are as reliable as random guessing [2]. I very much hope that the approach of combining attribution and feature visualization can be backed up by quantitative, falsifiable evaluation. If it cannot, I would be inclined to question its inclusion in the paper.

2. The paper makes the big assumption that feature visualizations which resemble natural images are better (explained above), which is a prior that's independent of the model and can thus lead to misleading visualizations if the assumption isn't met by the investigated model.

3. The claim that "we supply the field of XAI with a reliable tool for explaining the semantic ('what' information) of modern vision models" sounds a bit too strong in my ears. Given the well-known limitations of feature visualizations (e.g., hard to falsify [3], hard to quantify, unreliable [very recent work 4]), I think it might be worth formulating this a bit more carefully, for instance as "we supply the field of XAI with a tool for exploring hypotheses about the semantic ('what' information) of modern vision models".

[1] Zhou et al. https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/21196/20945

[2] Bilodeau et al. https://arxiv.org/pdf/2212.11870.pdf

[3] Leavitt & Morcos https://arxiv.org/pdf/2010.12016.pdf

[4] Geirhos et al. https://arxiv.org/pdf/2306.04719.pdf

QUESTIONS:
1. How can the feature attribution + feature visualization aspect be quantified and falsified? (see above)

2. For every single feature visualization figure shown in the paper and the supplementary materials, how were the units chosen? If at random, great; if not I would encourage the authors to either update those figures with randomly chosen classes (and channels etc.) or to state, in the figure caption, that they were not chosen randomly (a.k.a. cherry-picked).

3. Are you planning to open-source the technique & code, e.g. as a standalone library / contribution to an existing library to facilitate using the method? (Not required of course, but might be something worth thinking about)

I am willing to increase my score if my concerns can be addressed; in particular regarding feature attribution + feature visualization, random vs. cherry-picked visualizations, and toning down the claim a bit.

Minor:

3. In line 37, it would be useful to define 'faithful'

4. In lines 178-180, it would be useful to quantify this statement, e.g. in the appendix

LIMITATIONS:
Appendix A contains a great discussion on potential limitations, including the fact that "For a feature visualization to be truly informative about the model (e.g., about those spurious features), it might need to generate visualizations that stray away from the spectrum of natural images, and thus, yield worse scores using our metrics."
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
## Summary

This paper presents MACO, a simple and novel method to generate feature visualization for deep neural networks. MACO uses a constraint to optimize feature visualization in the space of natural images. It also presents an attribution-based mask to augment generated images to highlight spacial importance. To evaluate MACO, three metrics (transferability, plausibility, and alignment with natural images) are proposed. Evaluation study shows that MACO generates more useful feature visualization and is scalable to modern vision models such as ViT.

STRENGTHS:
## Strengths 

S1: This paper aims to improve the existing feature visualization method, which is popular in explaining vision models. This is a very critical and timely research topic.

S2: The proposed method MACO is sound, and the intuition of optimizing for image phase over magnitude is reasonable. It's good that MACO doesn't need to use any strong parametric image priors.

S3: The paper is well-written and easy to follow. I am looking forward to seeing the interactive visualization website!

WEAKNESSES:
## Major weaknesses

W1: The evaluation can be improved. The goal of feature visualization is to help human users interpret computer vision models. However, I don't see a strong connection between the proposed evaluation scores (plausibility, FID, and transferability scores) and explainability. These scores assume that *feature visualizations more similar to natural images are better at explaining ML models.* However,  this paper does not provide strong evidence that this hypothesis is true. This paper would be much stronger if it can directly highlight MACO improves explainability.

W2: Related to W1, a user study is necessary for this paper. I found some of the figures very hard to interpret. Studying how real users (e.g., ML developers and researchers) would interpret and use MACO-generated feature visualizations in their workflows would yield many interesting insights.

## Minor weaknesses

M1: The abstract can be improved by giving more details about the MACO technique. The current one-sentence, "It consists in optimizing solely an image's phase spectrum while keeping its magnitude constant to ensure that the generated explanations lie in the space of natural images." might be too abstract.

QUESTIONS:
1. How does MACO improve ML explainability?
2. How would an ML practitioner/researcher use MACO to improve their ML models?

LIMITATIONS:
Yes.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper proposes a small but apparently effective modification to the common technique of feature visualizations to improve the visual quality of the produced explanations. Specifically, this approach uses a different image parameterization producing images with power spectra similar to that of natural/dataset images.

STRENGTHS:
- The modification to the feature visualization procedure is well-motivated and described. The method is easy to understand.

WEAKNESSES:
- Section 4: The authors claim that they are the first to introduce quantitative methods for comparing feature visualizations. This is, however, incorrect as Borowski et al. [47] and Zimmermann et al. [48] already introduce evaluation schemes for measuring the helpfulness of visualizations.
- This study does not include any psychophysical evaluation/study investigating the helpfulness of the produced visualizations for humans. This is, however, a crucial part when developing new XAI methods as was noted by different earlier studies. I encourage the authors to test their method using the quantitative psychophysical benchmark introduced by Borowski et al. 2020, as this will drastically increase my confidence in this work and, hence, also my rating.
- While the authors list an interactive website as one of their contributions, we sadly cannot access and test it. While I understand that granting us access to this without risking a violation of the double-blind peer review process is difficult, I'm feeling uncomfortable giving the paper a great rating (based on this claimed contribution) without being able to review this website, too.

QUESTIONS:
- L161: The transformations used by Olah et al. can also be summarized as just a few, namely random cropping with zoom and rotations.
- L179: Showing a visual comparison would be appreciated.
- L202ff: Motivating why these augmentations are used/needed would be great. This sounds somehow contracting the earlier paragraphs where the authors say they want to remove these augmentations.
- L205f: This is a bit misleading; Olah et al. also didn't use explicit regularizers.
- L212: Unclear what is meant by confirmation bias in this context and how the proposed method addresses this.
- L216ff: Why is such a visualization more helpful/better for a user than the unmasked one? Performing a user study for this could be helpful.
- L233f: Using the plausibility score to judge a visualization appears to assume that the classifier is doing a great job and learns something that is perfectly aligned with how humans perform this classification.

LIMITATIONS:
- The authors do not discuss any limitations in the main part of their paper.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper proposes MACO, a feature visualization method which focuses the generation process of the visualizations on the image's phase spectrum while keeping the magnitude constant. This makes the generated explanation visualizations lie in the space of natural images, and hence, clearer for visual inspection.

In addition three metrics are proposed, i.e. plausability, FID and transferability, aimed at the quantitative comparison of the visualizations produced by feature visualization methods.

The proposed method is evaluated in several architectures, e.g. FlexiViT, ViT, ResNetV2, and compared against related methods that do not rely on image priors.

STRENGTHS:
- Presentation of the manuscript is good, some of the design choices are properly motivated.
- The proposed method is validated in a variety of architectures.
- The propose method effectively removes the need for hyperparameters and explicit regularization.

WEAKNESSES:
- W1: The proposed method for generating the explanation visualizations raises from the observation that human recognition of objects in images is driven not by magnitude but by phase (l.190). By following this principled aren't we assuming that the model being explained operates in the same manner as humans? aren't we risking hiding features potentially useful by the model and not necessarily following human-related processes?

- W2: The manuscript proposes three metrics/scores (plausibility, FID and transferability) to quantitatively compare the feature visualizations produced by different methods.

Plausability and FID, are related to the quantification of the similarly between the distribution of a given visualization (or set of visualizations) from a given class, and the distribution of natural images from that class. While the notion behind these two metrics is clear, especially for FID, I am not sure how sensitive these two metrics might be to trivial inputs, e.g. an average image of the class of interest? I would expect this input to get a very good value on the two. Perhaps a similar trend might be observed for methods that exploit image priors.

transferability, is related to the activations/prediction triggered by the visualizations under evaluation when fed to other models trained on the same datasets. In this regard, I fail to see how following this procedure will help 1) assess the validity of the visualization and 2) its relationship with respect to the model it was extracted from.

In l.262 it is claimed that the three proposed scores provide a more complete and accurate evaluation of the feature visualization methods, based on the observations made above, this statement might be inaccurate

- W3: Besides the quantitative evaluation using the three proposed metrics, with results reported in Table 1 and Table 2, all the other sources of validation are purely qualitative.

QUESTIONS:
- Please see W1
- What would the Plausability and FID metrics report on average images and methods that exploit image priors?
- What is the main motivation behind the transferability metric? In the way it is defined, how would it help assess the validity of the visualizations and its relationship with respect to the model it was extracted from?

LIMITATIONS:
- Visualizations somewhat biased towards human perception/recognition principles (risk of low fidelity to the model being interpreted)
- While a good first attempt and step on the right direction, the proposed quantitative metrics might not be the most optimal.
- Good part of the evaluation is qualitative.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper proposes a simple yet effective approach to visualize deep learning features without using a parametric image prior. Improving upon the prior work that uses the Fourier domain formulation, the proposed method uses the polar form and optimizes the image phase to maximize the activation of specified neurons. The rest of the pipeline is similar to the prior work in terms of the optimization procedure and the transparency mask estimation. The paper further designs two new metrics for feature visualizations, where the proposed method outperforms the prior arts. In addition to feature visualization, the proposed method is applied to feature inversion of single images or learned concepts.

STRENGTHS:
- The paper proposes a clever idea to optimize the phase of the image, which is commonly used for image editing, to make it look more natural for feature visualization. 

- The qualitative result comparison clearly shows the significant improvement of the proposed method over the prior arts without using parametric image priors.

- The paper proposes two new metrics to compare feature visualization methods from different perspectives.

- The paper is well-written and the figures are helpful for understanding.

WEAKNESSES:
Major
----
- Lack of significant novelty. The other two contributions claimed in the paper are less convincing: the second contribution is proposed in the SmoothGrad paper as acknowledged by the paper, and the third contribution on metrics isn't thorough in its analysis.

- Lack of thorough analysis of the proposed new metrics.
    - Overall, all inverted images without a parametric image prior look so different from natural images. Thus, it's unclear if it's meaningful to show which method is slightly better via the plausible and FID metric. It's different from using FID to judge image generation or style transfer results.
    - For the plausibility/FID metric, in addition to image appearance, the image content of feature inversion results are so different from natural images. As the proposed model aims to maximize the activation of a neuron at all spatial locations, the inverted images are a collage of discriminative object parts, which is not similar to natural images. It may be better to just maximize the neuron at the center spatial location to invert the feature into one object part. 
    - For the transferability score, the experiment is only done on 500 features, while ImageNet has 1,000 classes for the classification test. It's unclear how robust and reliable is the reported result. Note that there are 200 classes of dogs in ImageNet, and thus the transferability score may be different for fine-grain categories.

- Lack of comparison on scene image datasets ,e.g. PLACES. The phase of outdoor images are significantly different from indoor images. It'll be more convincing to also benchmark on scene image datasets.

- Lack of ablation study on the augmentation of the two transformations.

Minor
----
- Lack of runtime analysis. It's unclear if the proposed method is fast to run in practice

- Lack of analysis on the random seed. It'll be good to show the diversity of results using different random seeds.

QUESTIONS:
Are there any failure cases of the proposed method?

LIMITATIONS:
N/A
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (poster)

COMMENT:
The reviewers did not reach a consensus and are clustered around the borderline. We believe this paper brings more useful information to the community than not and hence accept this paper. For instance, making visualizations work for modern networks and the website itself are two worthy contributions. The lack of user study is indeed a downside, but we believe the pros outweigh the cons here.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
